jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence with Homer's commute
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation after what's presumably a car accident
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase delivery - perfectly character-integrated and economical, though not particularly original at this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Smell like Streep For cheap!
jokes.setup: Beauty pageant sponsorship announcement
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun combining Meryl Streep with cheap perfume marketing
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that fits Troy's cheesy spokesman character perfectly - the kind of awful pun that's so bad it's good

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such beauty pageants as 'Miss Universe: The Pageant' and 'Teen Steam: A Moisturizing Saga.' I'm here to announce our new fragrance line, 'Meryl Streep's Essence.' It's got notes of expensive sophistication, but it only costs $3.99. You might say it's a Meryl Streep... at Meryl's Price!

jokes.text: Token black panelist, Drederick Tatum
jokes.setup: Introducing pageant judges with their credentials
jokes.punchline: Blatantly acknowledging tokenism instead of pretending it doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Sharp social satire that cuts through TV's polite pretenses - economical and brave for its time

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Our judges tonight are: Professor Frink, inventor of the electric nose warmer; Dr. Hibbert, Springfield's most prominent physician; and this man, who is black.

jokes.text: Memo to Goldie Hawn: cheerleading tryouts were 30 years ago. Let's grow up, shall we?
jokes.setup: Fashion critic giving a preview of his worst-dressed list
jokes.punchline: Specific, mean-spirited critique of Goldie Hawn's youthful image
jokes.explanation: Good character work for the bitchy fashion critic - specific celebrity target makes it feel authentic

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Boswell
dialogue.line: Goldie Hawn... still trying to play the ingenue at her age. It's not cute, it's creepy. Those miniskirts, that giggle – it's like watching your grandmother do a cartwheel.

jokes.text: He's such a bitch.
jokes.setup: Mr. Boswell's catty comment about Goldie Hawn
jokes.punchline: Another judge's blunt assessment of his behavior
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction - the bluntness works in context

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That remark was completely uncalled for and inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Judge Constance Harm
dialogue.line: Yeah, what he said. Also, you're a jerk.

jokes.text: You know, I spend all day alone with Maggie... and sometimes it's like I don't even exist. - Sounds interesting.
jokes.setup: Marge opening up about feeling invisible and isolated
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely distracted, dismissive response showing he's not listening
jokes.explanation: Perfect character work - Homer's inattention proves Marge's point about feeling invisible. The irony is painful and funny

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I've been feeling so invisible lately. Like nobody really sees me or cares what I think.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm, that's nice, honey.

jokes.text: She, uh-- Good for her.
jokes.setup: Troy introducing Miss Delaware but having nothing to say
jokes.punchline: His awkward recovery with meaningless praise
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Troy's professional incompetence - the stammering feels authentic

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, Miss Delaware!

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: She's... she's very... Delaware-y.

jokes.text: And I'd like to apologize one last time... for my unfortunate remarks at the United Nations.
jokes.setup: Beauty pageant winner giving farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to some diplomatic incident we never learn about
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist comedy - the idea that a beauty queen caused an international incident is wonderfully ridiculous

jokes.text: Kids, back me up. - He's right, Mom. - Sorry. Match point-- Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming Marge never told him about the audition
jokes.punchline: The kids siding with Homer and his sports metaphor victory lap
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamics - Homer treating domestic arguments like sports competition is very in-character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge never told me about any audition!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dad's right. Mom totally kept that from you.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's true. She didn't mention it to any of us.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See? It's like I always say - if you're not in the game, you can't be benched! Woohoo!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad just won that argument.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And he didn't even have to yell.

jokes.text: Oh, indeedily-doodily. Uh, I've even been in Streetcar once before. I played Blanche DuBois. Mm-hmm. Just part of the fun Of going to an all-male school.
jokes.setup: Ned casually mentioning his theater experience
jokes.punchline: The revelation that he played Blanche at an all-male school, delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned moment - his cheerful delivery of something that should be embarrassing, plus the character integration is flawless

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I had quite a bit of theatrical experience back in my school days.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: In fact, I played Blanche in 'A Streetcar Named Desire' at an all-boys school.

jokes.text: I have directed three plays in my career... and I have had three heart attacks. That's how much I care. I'm planning for a fourth.
jokes.setup: Theater director establishing his credentials
jokes.punchline: The correlation between his plays and heart attacks, plus planning another one
jokes.explanation: Excellent character establishment - the dramatic director who takes theater so seriously it's literally killing him. Great escalation and quotable delivery

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: I've directed plays in three countries, two of them real. My productions have been attended by thousands. Tens of thousands if you count the people who died in the theater.

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: There's a definite correlation between my plays and heart attacks. But I'm not discouraged. In fact, I'm planning my most ambitious production yet.

jokes.text: While directing Hats Off to Hanukkah... I reduced more than one cast member to tears. Did I expect too much from fourth graders?
jokes.setup: Director explaining his demanding nature
jokes.punchline: Revealing he made fourth graders cry during a Hanukkah play
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the character - the specificity of 'Hats Off to Hanukkah' and fourth graders crying is perfectly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: I'm a very demanding director. Why, I once made fourth graders cry during a Hanukkah play.

jokes.text: The review, 'Play Enjoyed By All,' speaks for itself.
jokes.setup: Director justifying his harsh methods with fourth graders
jokes.punchline: Citing the most generic, meaningless review possible as validation
jokes.explanation: Perfect character delusion - he interprets the blandest possible review as vindication of his methods

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: You may think I'm tough, but I'm just preparing these children for the harsh realities of show business. Why, I once worked with a director who would make me cry every single day!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: And you know what? It made me a better actor. A critic once said my performance was 'adequate.'

jokes.text: Schnell! Schnell! Schnell!
jokes.setup: Director demanding men take off shirts for Stanley audition
jokes.punchline: Switching to German commands for no apparent reason
jokes.explanation: Shows the director's pretentious, over-the-top nature - the random German fits his affected personality

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: Now, gentlemen, remove your shirts.

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: Entfernen Sie Ihre Hemden!

jokes.text: Oh, ye gods!
jokes.setup: Director sees someone who shocks him
jokes.punchline: His theatrical exclamation of horror
jokes.explanation: The theatrical language fits the pompous director character - sets up Homer's reveal

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: GAHHHHH!

jokes.text: Hey, man, if you like that, you should see my butt.
jokes.setup: Director's horrified reaction to seeing Homer shirtless
jokes.punchline: Homer's oblivious, inappropriate response offering to show more
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer obliviousness - he completely misreads the director's horror as appreciation

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? You want to see more? I got more where that came from.

jokes.text: All right. I'll pick up a bucket Of fried chicken, extra skin... rolls, chocolate cream parfait--
jokes.setup: Homer offering to comfort disappointed Marge
jokes.punchline: His idea of comfort food getting increasingly excessive and unhealthy
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer - his solution to emotional problems is excessive junk food, escalating to absurd levels

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Marge. I'll cheer you up with some comfort food.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: First, a box of donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then, a whole cake.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then, deep-fried butter.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then, a bucket of lard with a spoon.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... lard.

jokes.text: Stop bothering my Blanche!
jokes.setup: Homer listing comfort foods to console Marge
jokes.punchline: Director's dramatic intervention to protect his newly discovered star
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - the director's possessive, dramatic intervention perfectly captures his intensity and pretension

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... donuts, beer, pork chops, eggs...

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: No! You're ruining her! Step away from Marge this instant!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: She is a vessel of theatrical genius and I won't have her clogged up with your pedestrian comfort foods!

jokes.text: I feel like Lucie Arnaz Luckinbill.
jokes.setup: Lisa expressing pride in her mother becoming an actress
jokes.punchline: Referencing the daughter of a famous actress with an overly specific full name
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa character work - her intellectual reference is both specific and slightly pretentious, very age-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm so proud of Mom for becoming an actress!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: She's just like Marlon Brando's daughter, Cheyenne Brando.

jokes.text: Are there any jive-talking robots in this play?
jokes.setup: Family discussing Marge's theatrical role
jokes.punchline: Bart's completely inappropriate question about the serious drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - his complete misunderstanding of what theater is, filtered through his love of cartoons and robots

jokes.text: Is there any frontal nudity?
jokes.setup: Family asking questions about Marge's play
jokes.punchline: Homer's inappropriate question about nudity
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer inappropriateness - predictable but fits the character perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Does Marge get naked in this play?

jokes.text: My name is 'Ot-to!' I'm playing 'Pab-lo!'
jokes.setup: Cast introductions continuing
jokes.punchline: Otto's enthusiastic pronunciation emphasizing syllables in both names
jokes.explanation: Fits Otto's laid-back, dramatic personality - the syllable emphasis shows his theatrical enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Otto Man!

jokes.text: Well, would anyone else like a bite of banality?
jokes.setup: Marge offering peanut butter brownies to cast
jokes.punchline: Director's pretentious rejection using 'banality' as wordplay with food
jokes.explanation: Excellent character writing - the director's pretentious vocabulary applied to rejecting homemade brownies is perfectly pompous

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Would you like a peanut butter brownie?

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: Peanut butter? How... banal. I only consume foods of the utmost sophistication. Why, just this morning I had a single, locally-sourced pomegranate seed paired with a 1947 Château d'Yquem.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh.

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: Though I must say, the banality of your brownie is only surpassed by the banality of your... everything.

jokes.text: Aww, isn't that cute! And me without a camera.
jokes.setup: Homer roughly handling Marge during rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Day care worker's inappropriate, cheerful reaction to domestic violence simulation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor - the worker's oblivious cheerfulness about what looks like abuse creates uncomfortable comedy

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's so realistic! Do it again, do it again!

jokes.text: She's saying, 'I am a leech.'
jokes.setup: Marge asking what babies mean when they reach for bottles
jokes.punchline: Harsh interpretation of natural baby behavior as parasitic dependency
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - taking innocent baby behavior and giving it the harshest possible psychological interpretation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What do babies mean when they reach for bottles?

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: They're trying to extract resources from you without contributing anything back. Classic parasitic behavior.

jokes.text: but we're the only day care center in town... that's not currently under investigation by the state.
jokes.setup: Day care worker promoting their facility
jokes.punchline: Their main selling point is not being investigated (implying everyone else is)
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - the lowest possible bar for childcare quality becomes their competitive advantage

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: We're the only day care in Springfield that's not currently under investigation.

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: Well, not currently. We were last month, but we got off on a technicality.

jokes.text: Hate to be an armchair Blanche... but I always gave it one of these-- There. There's the ol' face-shredder.
jokes.setup: Homer offering acting advice to Marge
jokes.punchline: His casual reference to 'armchair Blanche' and calling the bottle move a 'face-shredder'
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - treating theatrical violence like sports moves, plus the 'armchair Blanche' wordplay is clever

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Listen Marge, you gotta commit to the character. Be like an armchair Blanche - you know, lounging around, sultry, mysterious.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And when you pick up that bottle, really sell it. That's what I call the face-shredder - gets 'em every time.

jokes.text: I'm, uh-- I'm gonna crawl into bed with a bottle of amaretto. Good day.
jokes.setup: Director disgusted by the rehearsal quality
jokes.punchline: His dramatic exit involving alcoholic self-medication
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - the director's theatrical despair leads to specific, pathetic self-destruction

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: This is a travesty! Your performances are an embarrassment to the theatrical arts!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: I cannot work under these conditions. I'm leaving!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: And I'm taking my dignity... and this bottle of wine.

jokes.text: 7-1 0 split.
jokes.setup: Homer bowling while Marge wants to practice lines
jokes.punchline: The bowling alley announcing Homer's difficult split
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing Homer's priorities - bowling takes precedence over helping Marge with her art

dialogue.speaker: Electronic Voice
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson, 7-10 split.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why don't you come home and help me practice my lines?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Can't, Marge. I'm on a roll.

jokes.text: you forgot that other people have problems too.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining why he can't help Marge with lines
jokes.punchline: His complete obliviousness to the irony of his statement while bowling
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic comedy - Homer accusing Marge of selfishness while literally playing games instead of helping her

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I can't help you with your lines, Marge. I'm too busy bowling.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're not even bowling right now.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but I might bowl later, and I need to prepare mentally.

jokes.text: Don't like to nap, eh? We have a place for babies like you-- the box!
jokes.setup: Maggie refusing to nap at day care
jokes.punchline: The worker's sinister threat of punishment isolation
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about childcare abuse - 'the box' as punishment is both threatening and absurd

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: Maggie, it's nap time. You need to sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: If you don't take a nap, I'm going to have to put you in the quiet room... with the OTHER babies.

jokes.text: All right, let 'ham-ateur' night in Dixie commence.
jokes.setup: Director beginning rehearsal
jokes.punchline: His pretentious pun combining 'ham' and 'amateur' with Southern reference
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - the director's theatrical wordplay shows his pretentious nature while insulting the cast

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: Now, thespians, I know you're all very excited to be here, but let's not get ahead of ourselves. Some of you are nothing but ham-ateurs. Or as we say in the South, you're a bunch of ham-fisted yokels.

jokes.text: Couldn't she just take his abuse with gentle good humor?
jokes.setup: Marge questioning why Blanche should fight back with bottle
jokes.punchline: Her suggestion to passively accept abuse with good humor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character work - her instinct to avoid confrontation even in fictional scenarios reflects her real-life patterns

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why should you fight back with a bottle? You should just smile and take it like a good wife.

jokes.text: Marge, every second you spend with this man... he is crushing your fragile spirit. You can't let that happen.
jokes.setup: Director coaching Marge about Stanley
jokes.punchline: His description perfectly applies to Marge's real marriage with Homer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary - the director unknowingly describes Marge's actual life situation while talking about the play

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: You must play Stanley as a man trapped in a loveless, sexless marriage, a man whose dreams have been crushed by years of disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my...

jokes.text: I'm trying. I'm trying. [Straining]
jokes.setup: Director telling Ned to overpower Marge in the scene
jokes.punchline: Ned's ineffectual attempts to be physically dominating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned character work - his inability to be aggressive or dominating, even in acting

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Okay, Ned, in this scene you need to overpower Marge. Really dominate her.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, okay-diddly-okay! I'll give it the old college try-erino!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: En garde, Marge! Prepare to be... uh... firmly encouraged!

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Ned, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: I'm dominating you! Pow! Pow! Pow! Oh, I'm sorry, did that hurt? I can do it softer.

jokes.text: An' I'll talk like 'is. Bob's yer uncle, mate.
jokes.setup: Family trying to support Marge's Southern accent
jokes.punchline: Bart adopting a random Cockney accent instead
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - his complete misunderstanding of the assignment, choosing the wrong accent entirely

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oi, guv'nor! Blimey, me mum's got a lovely accent, innit?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, what the hell are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm supporting Mom's Southern accent, man.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, that's not Southern, that's Cockney.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Right you are, old chap!

jokes.text: I'm livin' in a cuckoo clock!
jokes.setup: Family all speaking in different accents
jokes.punchline: Homer's frustrated metaphor for his chaotic household
jokes.explanation: Great Homer metaphor - the cuckoo clock image perfectly captures the mechanical chaos of his home life

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is it like herding cats around here? I'm surrounded by lunatics!

jokes.text: For God's sakes, you can pull the lid off your own can of pudding!
jokes.setup: Homer asking about dessert
jokes.punchline: Marge's exasperated response showing her growing assertiveness
jokes.explanation: Good character development moment - Marge's newfound assertiveness emerging through mundane domestic tasks

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, what's for dessert?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I've made dinner, done the laundry, helped Bart with homework, and listened to you talk about donuts for twenty minutes. If you want dessert, there's a kitchen right there!

jokes.text: My pudding is trapped forever! So I can open my own can Of pudding, can I? Shows what you know, Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to prove his independence with pudding
jokes.punchline: His immediate failure and bitter sarcasm toward absent Marge
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his prideful incompetence and talking to absent Marge shows his childlike dependency

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need Marge! I can take care of myself. I'll make my own pudding!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... pudding. Why won't you set?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, who am I kidding? Marge, where are you?! I can't do anything right without you!

jokes.text: Keep yelling, you big ape.
jokes.setup: Homer calling for Marge
jokes.punchline: Marge's dismissive, insulting response showing her changed attitude
jokes.explanation: Strong character development - Marge calling Homer an 'ape' shows her growing resentment and assertiveness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What is it, you idiot?

jokes.text: Oh, wait, there is.
jokes.setup: Marge saying there's nothing about bowling in the play
jokes.punchline: Homer's realization that there actually is bowling in Streetcar
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the actual play - Homer's only interest is validated by Stanley's bowling hobby

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. There's bowling in Streetcar?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, why didn't you tell me that?!

jokes.text: 'Cause I don't care, okay? I can't fake an interest in this... and I'm an expert at faking an interest in your kooky projects.
jokes.setup: Marge asking why Homer isn't supportive
jokes.punchline: Homer's brutally honest admission about faking interest in her activities
jokes.explanation: Excellent character writing - Homer's accidentally devastating honesty about his marriage patterns is both funny and painful

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why aren't you ever supportive of the things I do?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I've been pretending to care about your activities for years. I don't even know what you do half the time. I just nod and say 'mmm-hmm' while thinking about donuts.

jokes.text: Hello, Maggie. Poor little 'dummard.'
jokes.setup: Day care worker greeting Maggie condescendingly
jokes.punchline: Made-up word combining 'dummy' and some suffix
jokes.explanation: Cruel day care worker's made-up insult for babies

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: Well, hello there, you little dummox!

jokes.text: If I break, buy a new one. [Laughs]
jokes.setup: Maggie playing with what appears to be a dangerous toy
jokes.punchline: Label suggesting babies are disposable consumer products
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about disposable consumer culture applied to children - the casualness makes it more disturbing and funny

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Warning: Choking hazard for children under 8 years.

dialogue.speaker: Label
dialogue.line: Not responsible for injuries, death, or existential dread. Babies are disposable.

jokes.text: [Shudders] Babies.
jokes.setup: Apparent chaos and destruction caused by babies at day care
jokes.punchline: Worker's disgusted, defeated reaction to her chosen profession
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - the ultimate expression of someone who chose the wrong career path

dialogue.speaker: Day care worker
dialogue.line: I went to college for this.

jokes.text: Except you. You're not working out. I'll be playing your part.
jokes.setup: Director's inspirational speech to transform suburbanites into entertainers
jokes.punchline: Immediately firing someone and taking their role
jokes.explanation: Perfect director characterization - his inspirational moment immediately undercut by petty, ego-driven decision

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: You are no longer the shuffling midgets you were five minutes ago. You are now... entertainers!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: You, sir, are finished!

dialogue.speaker: Llewellyn Sinclair
dialogue.line: I'm taking your part.

jokes.text: # New Orleans # # Stinking, rotten 'vomity' vile #
jokes.setup: Musical number about New Orleans
jokes.punchline: The made-up word 'vomity' in an otherwise sophisticated song
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay - inventing 'vomity' as an adjective in a song that's trying to be sophisticated creates amusing contrast

dialogue.speaker: Chorus
dialogue.line: New Orleans, home of pirates and voodoo, where the swamps are misty and the air is vomity.

jokes.text: Cool. She can fly.
jokes.setup: Marge being lifted away in the play's climactic moment
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual, impressed reaction to the theatrical effect
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - his matter-of-fact reaction to theatrical artifice, seeing it as literally flying

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa.

jokes.text: Hey, look at me. I'm Blanche DuBois.
jokes.setup: Post-show celebration
jokes.punchline: Homer presumably doing a silly impression of Blanche
jokes.explanation: Basic Homer physical comedy - his attempt to join in the theatrical celebration in his crude way

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm Blanche DuBois, and I do declare, I'm fixin' to have the vapors!

jokes.text: Lookin' for a spot off un with the missus, hey, guv'nor?
jokes.setup: Homer wanting to talk privately with Marge
jokes.punchline: Bart still using his Cockney accent from earlier
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to Bart's earlier Cockney accent experiment - shows his persistence with the bit

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I need to talk to you privately.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oi, gov'nor! Can't a bloke join in on the conversation?

jokes.text: The poor thing ends up being hauled to the nuthouse... when all she needed was for that big slob to show her some respect.
jokes.setup: Homer analyzing the play's meaning
jokes.punchline: His unknowing self-description as 'that big slob' who should show respect
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-comedy - Homer perfectly analyzing his own behavior patterns without realizing it, showing genuine emotional growth

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, look at this! 'That big slob should show more respect.' Hey, that's me!

jokes.text: I have a history of missing the point of stuff like this.
jokes.setup: Homer giving thoughtful analysis of the play
jokes.punchline: His self-deprecating acknowledgment of his usual obtuseness
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Homer's rare moment of self-awareness about his intellectual limitations

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, this play really makes you think. For once, I actually understood what was going on. Of course, by tomorrow I'll have forgotten everything and go back to being my usual dumb self.

jokes.text: Yeah, like when I pick my teeth with the mail and stuff.
jokes.setup: Homer comparing himself to Stanley
jokes.punchline: His specific, disgusting habit as evidence of similarity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer specificity - the disgusting detail of using mail to pick teeth shows his complete lack of refinement

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm just like Stanley. We're practically the same person.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: How are you like Stanley?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we both pick our noses and eat it.
